one_line_takeaway: TextSeal extends Gumbel-max LLM watermarking with dual-key routing (restoring generation diversity while staying distortion-free and speculative-decoding/MTP-compatible), entropy-weighted Gamma-calibrated detection, and dyadic geometric-cover localized detection, and empirically dominates SynthID-Text on the diversity-detectability frontier, near-zero sampling overhead, negligible quality/human-perceptible impact, robust detection under heavy dilution/fragmentation, and radioactive signal transfer through distillation for unauthorized-distillation detection.
key_finding: TextSeal strictly dominates SynthID-Text and Gumbel-Max on the diversity-detectability Pareto frontier (Self-BLEU vs. -log10 p, Qwen3.5-27B, ELI5, 400 tokens). On the 12-benchmark suite it preserves average accuracy (70.6 vs 70.6 at T=0.6 with vs without watermark; 69.1 vs 69.3 at T=1.0), with a minor 1-2 point drop on code benchmarks (HumanEval 93.3 vs 97.0, MBPP 49.2 vs 50.2 at T=0.6) attributed to formatting omissions rather than reasoning failures. Human evaluation (6,000 A/B pairs, 5 languages) found no significant preference difference (overall net win rate +0.4%, p=0.54; TOST equivalence established overall and for 4/5 languages at Delta=5%, Arabic marginal p=0.06). Under dilution, localized ensemble detection maintains -log10 p > 4 at 12,000-token documents (3.3% watermarked fraction, 30x dilution) while global detection fails below the p=0.01 threshold beyond ~4,000 tokens; under fragmentation, ensemble detection succeeds up to K=3 fragments but degrades at K=5 (~80-token fragments). Sampling overhead is <=0.3% (~0.07ms/token) versus SynthID's 0.4-1.1% (~0.6ms/token) on H200 GPUs across model sizes 0.8B-27B. Entropy-weighted detection with a 4-bit quantized 0.8B proxy model achieves near-parity detectability (median -log10 p = 6.2) versus the full 27B proxy (8.2) and unweighted baseline (4.8), at only 1.4GB VRAM and 0.115ms/token vs 50.1GB/0.213ms for the 27B proxy. On GPT-OSS-20B multilingual generalization (6,000 paired samples), TextSeal achieves 63.3% TPR at 0.1% FPR overall, with no significant refusal-rate or script-consistency differences (McNemar's p=0.41, p=0.21). Radioactivity experiments show all three watermarks (Gumbel-Max, TextSeal, SynthID) transfer detectably through LoRA distillation into a student model; under original (unequalized) data volume TextSeal gives the strongest signal because more of its traces pass the quality filter (47.0% retained vs 39.8% for Gumbel-Max), but under equalized data volume (equal traces or equal tokens) Gumbel-Max dominates per-token signal strength due to its fully deterministic argmax selection. Distilled students improve substantially over the base Qwen2.5-3B on GSM8K (64.5% baseline vs 78.8-79.9% across watermark conditions, +10.7 to +15.4 points), with no significant accuracy difference between watermarked and unwatermarked-control distillation.
core_contribution: TextSeal (Meta FAIR / Meta Superintelligence Labs) is a distortion-free LLM text watermark built on the Gumbel-max scheme, contributing three original mechanisms: (1) Dual-Key Routing/Generation, which randomly alternates between two secret keys k(1) and k(2) (routing probability alpha in [0,0.5]) during Gumbel-max token selection to restore output diversity that standard (single-key) Gumbel-max lacks, while natively supporting speculative decoding and multi-token prediction (MTP) by mapping the two keys onto draft-accepted vs. resampled tokens; (2) Entropy-Weighted Detection, which weights each token's watermark score by its local entropy (estimated via a single forward pass of a smaller/quantized proxy model), using a moment-matched Gamma approximation to obtain calibrated p-values for the resulting weighted sum of non-identical exponentials; and (3) Multi-Region Localized Detection, a geometric cover search over dyadic window lengths that greedily extracts disjoint watermarked intervals from a document and fuses global/single-window/multi-region tests via a flat Bonferroni-corrected (k=3) adaptive ensemble, enabling detection under dilution and fragmentation where global detectors fail. The paper also demonstrates (Section 6) that TextSeal's watermark signal is 'radioactive': it transfers through model distillation (teacher-student, reasoning-trace distillation) and can be detected in the student via an open-model teacher-forcing radioactivity test, enabling detection of unauthorized model distillation.
threat_model: Provenance/detection setting: a detector holding the secret key(s) wants to determine (a) whether a piece of text was generated by the watermarked LLM, including when the watermarked content is diluted or fragmented inside a larger human-authored or mixed document, and (b) whether a third-party 'student' model was trained (distilled) on outputs of the watermarked LLM without authorization, by testing for inherited PRF-token bias in the student via teacher forcing. The paper does not model an adversary trying to break/remove the watermark through paraphrasing/rewriting attacks in a rigorous adversarial-robustness sense within the main text shown (attack-strength curves such as copy-edit/light rephrase/selective rephrase/full rewrite appear in Figure 6 as robustness checks for the entropy-weighted proxy-model detector, but are not analyzed as a formal adversarial threat model in the reviewed sections).
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: like all distortion-free sampling watermarks, TextSeal trades output diversity (not quality) for detectability; this may matter for workflows relying on diverse outputs (best-of-N reranking, creative brainstorming), though the authors note modern RL-trained reasoning models already exhibit collapsed entropy, limiting the marginal diversity loss, and note that quantifying this tradeoff across model families remains open. Additional limitations observable from the text: entropy-weighted detection with a strong proxy model requires meaningfully more compute/VRAM (up to 50.1GB and 0.213ms/token for a 27B proxy) even though a 4-bit 0.8B proxy recovers most of the benefit; multi-region localization degrades once fragments become small (K=5, ~80 tokens/fragment) due to multiple-testing penalties; and the radioactivity/distillation results depend on a curated, quality-filtered distillation setup (four-stage filter: closed think-block, boxed answer, matches reference via math_verify, no 100-character repeated span) which may not represent all real-world distillation pipelines. Robustness to active removal attacks (paraphrasing/rewriting) is only shown as detectability curves for the entropy-weighted proxy-model ablation (Figure 6, attack strengths from copy-edit to full rewrite) rather than as a dedicated adversarial-robustness study in the sections reviewed.

datasets_used: ELI5 (Fan et al. 2019) - 1,000 prompts for main detectability/diversity experiments, 2,000 English samples for human evaluation, 400-token truncation
CaLMQA (Arora et al. 2025) - 1,000 samples each for Arabic, Chinese, Hindi, Japanese in human evaluation and multilingual generalization experiments
1 million unwatermarked Wikipedia passages (256 tokens each) for empirical FPR calibration
Wikipedia texts (varying length up to 12,000 tokens) for dilution/fragmentation localization experiments
OpenR1-Math-220k (Hugging Face 2025) - 5,000 curated problems used for teacher-student distillation
GSM8K (1,319 problems, greedy decoding) - used to measure distilled student accuracy
12-benchmark suite for downstream performance: AIME, MATH, GSM8K, HumanEval, MBPP, ARC-C, ARC-E, GPQA, HellaSwag, MMLU, SimpleQA, WinoGrande
quantitative_results: Average accuracy across 12 benchmarks: 70.6 (watermarked) vs 70.6 (no watermark) at T=0.6; 69.1 vs 69.3 at T=1.0
HumanEval accuracy drop at T=0.6: 93.3 (WM) vs 97.0 (no WM); MBPP: 49.2 vs 50.2

Human A/B eval (6,000 pairs): overall WM preference rate 50.9%, net win rate +0.4%, p=0.54; ties in 79% of samples; TOST equivalence established overall (90% CI [-0.6%, +1.4%] within +/-5%) and for 4/5 languages (Arabic p=0.06)
Dilution: localized ensemble maintains -log10 p > 4 at 12,000 tokens (3.3% watermarked); global detection falls below p=0.01 significance beyond ~4,000 tokens
Fragmentation: ensemble detects up to K=3 fragments (~133 tokens each within an 8,000-token document); degrades at K=5 (~80 tokens/fragment)
Sampling overhead: TextSeal <=0.3% (~0.07ms extra per token) vs SynthID 0.4-1.1% (~0.6ms), measured across Qwen 3.5 model sizes 0.8B-27B on H200 GPU

Entropy-weighted detection with proxy models: unweighted baseline median -log10 p = 4.8 (0.007ms/token, 0GB overhead); 4-bit quantized 0.8B proxy = 6.2 (0.115ms/token, 1.4GB VRAM); full 27B proxy = 8.2 (0.213ms/token, 50.1GB VRAM)

Multilingual generalization (GPT-OSS-20B, 6,000 paired samples): 63.3% TPR at 0.1% FPR overall; refusal rates <1% for both conditions (McNemar p=0.41); script consistency >98% except Japanese ~90% (McNemar p=0.21)

Radioactivity/distillation (original, unequalized data): teacher trace retention rates - Gumbel-Max 39.8% (1,991 traces), TextSeal 47.0% (2,352 traces), SynthID 48.2% (2,408 traces), Control 48.0% (2,400 traces); teacher detection -log10(p): Gumbel-Max 14.89, TextSeal 33.15 (entropy-weighted), SynthID 14.39, Control 0.39
Distilled student GSM8K accuracy (base Qwen2.5-3B = 64.5%): Gumbel-Max 78.8% (+14.3), TextSeal 79.9% (+15.4), SynthID 75.2% (+10.7), Control 75.5% (+11.0)
Controlled equal-traces comparison (1,991 traces each): student accuracy TextSeal 81.0%, SynthID 78.8%, Control 78.8%, Gumbel-Max 77.7%
Controlled equal-tokens comparison (~15.1M characters each): TextSeal 79.7%, Gumbel-Max 78.6%, SynthID 79.6%, Control 77.6%
Entropy-weighting ablation for radioactivity detection: best (sqrt of H) weighting p=3.7e-110 vs uniform baseline p=2.1e-84, >25 orders of magnitude improvement
MTP speculative decoding: draft acceptance rates unchanged (29-46%) with vs without TextSeal across Qwen 3.5 2B/9B/27B; perplexity identical across conditions
baselines_compared: Gumbel-Max watermarking (Aaronson and Kirchner 2023) - the deterministic single-key scheme TextSeal builds on

SynthID-Text (Dathathri et al. 2024) - tournament-sampling watermark deployed in Google Gemini; compared at depth 10 (main experiments) and depths 2-20 (Pareto frontier sweep); frequentist Z-test detector used (Bayesian detector explicitly excluded due to lack of controlled FPR and incompatibility with localized multi-window testing)

Alternative diversity-restoration strategies (ablation, Section 5.1): Stochastic Mixing, Periodic Skip, Entropy-Normalized Skip, and Dual-Key with Fisher-style aggregation, all compared against TextSeal's early-fusion Dual-Key Routing
